coredata.dc:title: Superior photoelectrocatalytic performance of ternary structural BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 heterojunction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

coredata.dc:description: Herein, we performed an encyclopedic analysis on the photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen production of BiVO4/g-C3N4 decorated with reduced graphene oxide (RGO) or graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The differences between RGO and GQDs as an electron mediator was revealed for the first time in the perspective of theoretical DFT analysis and experimental validation. It was found that the incorporation of GQDs as an electron mediator promotes better photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen performance in comparison to the RGO. The addition of GQD can significantly improve the activity by 25.2 and 75.7% in comparison to the BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 and binary composite samples, respectively. Correspondingly, the BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 attained the highest photocurrent density of 19.2 mA/cm2 with an ABPE of 0.57% without the presence of any sacrificial reagents. This enhancement is stemming from the low photocharge carrier transfer resistance which was further verified via DFT study. The DFT analysis revealed that the BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 sample shared their electronic cloud density through orbital hybridization while the BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 sample show less mutual sharing. Additionally, the charge redistribution of the GQDs-composite at the heterostructure interface articulates a more stable and stronger heterojunction than the RGO-composite. Notably, this study provides new insights on the effect of different carbonaceous materials (RGO and GQDs) which are often used as an electron mediator to enhance photocatalytic activity.

head.ce:title: Superior photoelectrocatalytic performance of ternary structural BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 heterojunction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we performed an encyclopedic analysis on the photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen production of BiVO4/g-C3N4 decorated with reduced graphene oxide (RGO) or graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The differences between RGO and GQDs as an electron mediator was revealed for the first time in the perspective of theoretical DFT analysis and experimental validation. It was found that the incorporation of GQDs as an electron mediator promotes better photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen performance in comparison to the RGO. The addition of GQD can significantly improve the activity by 25.2 and 75.7% in comparison to the BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 and binary composite samples, respectively. Correspondingly, the BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 attained the highest photocurrent density of 19.2 mA/cm2 with an ABPE of 0.57% without the presence of any sacrificial reagents. This enhancement is stemming from the low photocharge carrier transfer resistance which was further verified via DFT study. The DFT analysis revealed that the BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 sample shared their electronic cloud density through orbital hybridization while the BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 sample show less mutual sharing. Additionally, the charge redistribution of the GQDs-composite at the heterostructure interface articulates a more stable and stronger heterojunction than the RGO-composite. Notably, this study provides new insights on the effect of different carbonaceous materials (RGO and GQDs) which are often used as an electron mediator to enhance photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:para: The burgeoning demand for rapid industrialization had exerted momentous pressure on the global environment. The onslaught of environmental pollution stemming from the overreliance of fossil fuels has provoked environmental threats that could affect the human being [1,2]. Thus, it is imperative to design a new energy technology via the utilization of renewable and sustainable resources in order to minimize the aforementioned environmental threats before it becomes irrevocable issues. Drawing inspiration from natural photosynthesis, photocatalytic water splitting via photocatalyst materials for the production of sustainable hydrogen energy has been regarded as a judicious strategy for future energy generation [3].

Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) has galvanized heightened interest among the scientific community as a promising material with peculiar photocatalytic features including moderate bandgap energy, excellent photochemical stability and environmentally friendly materials [4,5]. Generally, BiVO4 exists in three different crystal structures which are monoclinic clinobisvanite, orthorhombic pucherite and tetragonal dreyerite in which all of these structures possess different photocatalytic activity upon light illumination [6]. For example, the monoclinic structure was composed of additional Bi 6 s orbitals in the valance band which allows a short transition of photocharge carriers to the V 3d orbitals in the conduction band and thus corresponds to the smaller bandgap energy. Meanwhile, the tetragonal structure was made up of only O 2p orbitals, consequently, lengthen the transition of the photocharge carrier which can promote the recombination rate and limits the photocatalytic performance. Nevertheless, there are several bottleneck issues of the pure BiVO4 which has hindered the overall practicality of this semiconductor material [7,8].

Hitherto, various efforts have been performed in mitigating the inherent limitations of BiVO4 such as the formation of the heterostructure systems, introducing defects structures and nanoscaling [9]. Amidst the proposed strategies, the heterostructure system approach has been regarded as a promising strategy to significantly boost the photocatalytic performance owing to its smooth photocharge separation and migration; and limited photocharge recombination [10,11]. Recently, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) photocatalyst has drawn prime attention in the area of photocatalyst as a metal-free polymeric two-dimensional (2D) materials with medium bandgap energy [12,13]. The structural advantages of the g-C3N4 such as high thermal stability, direct bandgap and appropriate flat band potential for hydrogen evolution suggest a complementary heterostructure combination with the BiVO4 [11,14]. For example, Wang et al. [15] developed g-C3N4/BiVO4 photocatalyst with a high photocurrent density of 0.44 mA/cm2 at 0.56 V in the linear sweep current–voltage test. Subsequently, Safaei et al. [16] revealed that the g-C3N4/BiVO4 achieved high photocurrent density of 0.42 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE which was attributed to the lower charge transfer resistance and high oxygen vacancy sites. However, the g-C3N4/BiVO4 sample often possesses the Type II heterostructure system which has limited photocharge carrier redox abilities and not favourable for solar hydrogen production as it requires high redox potential of H2/H+. Recently, we have reported the promising potential of RGO as an electron mediator for mitigating the limitation that exists within the Type II system [17]. The addition of RGO has modified the Type II system into the novel Z-scheme system which possesses strong oxidation and reduction capability between the sample and fulfils the prerequisite of the solar hydrogen production [18,19].

Notwithstanding the promising potential of the aforementioned ternary system, the discussion on the effect of the different geometrical structures of the graphene-based materials, particularly RGO and GQDs on enhancing the heterostructure photocatalytic performances are often not been exhaustively scrutinized. Additionally, to the extent of authors’ knowledge, the study on photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen evolution using natural lake water without sacrificial reagents instead of the chemical-based electrolyte solution is limited.

Due to these Achilles’ heels, our group aims to provide an insight into all of these unanswerable questions by developing the BiVO4/g-C3N4 heterostructure photocatalyst with the addition of RGO or GQD for the first time. We evaluate the ternary structural differences via photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen production using natural lake water. The differences between both RGO and GQD as electron mediators were revealed in the view of experimental and DFT analysis. We systematically analyse the structural properties of each of the studied photocatalyst either in the pure form, binary structure and ternary heterostructure system. The electronic properties of these models are simulated to countercheck our experimental data. Simulated electronic properties include band structure (valence band, conduction band, and bandgap), the partial density of states (PDOS), Fermi energy level, electrostatic potential, charge difference density (CDD), surface formation energy and adsorption energy of water molecule.

The details of the purity and assay of the raw materials used in the synthesizing of the photocatalysts have been included in Supplementary Information. Generally, the pure BiVO4 and g-C3N4 were individually prepared via a modified hydrothermal method and thermal polycondensation of urea, respectively. The details of these synthesizing protocols can be found elsewhere [7]. Meanwhile, the RGO was synthesized using a modified Hummer’s method reported by Samsudin et al. [17] and GQDs were prepared according to the previously reported literature [20]. For the binary BiVO4/g-C3N4 sample, a wet-impregnation technique was applied to prepare this composite in which 0.8 wt% of g-C3N4 was thoroughly mixed with 1 g of BiVO4 in 40 mL of deionized water. Details of the procedure have been reported previously [7]. The same synthesis protocols were applied in preparing the ternary BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 and BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 samples in which each of the ternary structure contains 0.8 wt% of the graphene-based materials.

The physicochemical properties of the photocatalysts were thoroughly examined via X-ray diffraction (XRD), Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra, Field-Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) and High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) in which details of these measurements can be found in the Supplementary Information. Meanwhile, the PEC analysis was performed in a standard three-cell configuration system using an Autolab potentiostat PGSTAT302N (Metrohm). The fabrication method for preparing the photoanode (working electrode) containing as-developed photocatalyst was detailed in our previous publication [21]. Meanwhile, the reference and counter electrodes were made up of Ag/AgCl and Platinum rod. Additionally, 0.5 M Na2SO4 and 500 W halogen lamp (AM 1.5G illumination) were employed as an electrolyte and visible-light-source. Furthermore, the I-V curves were measured using a Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) procedure while the EIS was analyzed using a frequency response analyzer (FRA). Specific details on the measurement have been described previously [7].

The photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen production was measured in a PEC-DSSCs setup as illustrated in Fig. S1. The working anode was made up of the as-developed photoanode and the counter electrode was made up of platinum rod which was connected with DSSCs in a series arrangement to provide external bias energy. A 200 mL of natural lake water with pH 7.2 was used as a water source. The produced hydrogen analysed using Multiple Gas Analyzer software (SRI Instruments 8610–0071) equipped with TCD and a molecular sieve 13 X columns.

DFT calculations were performed on QuantumATK and the results were visualized on VESTA and Virtual NanoLab Version 2019.12. The details on the lattice parameters, hall symmetry, slab model calculations for surface energies and band edge locations, and other related DFT protocols for the pure BiVO4 and g-C3N4 has been well described in our recent paper [7]. Similarly, the protocols for measuring the surface formation energy and electrostatic potentials were included in our previous reports [16,22,23]. Meanwhile, in the case of RGO, GQDs, and g-C3N4, we used a single layer structure for the aforementioned analysis. Furthermore, in order to represent and counterchecked the experimental data, we built the following models of photocatalyst which include the BiVO4/GQDs, BiVO4/RGO, BiVO4/g-C3N4, BiVO4/GQDs/g-C3N4, and BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 heterostructures. The single layer of RGO, GQD, and g-C3N4 were adsorbed on the optimized surface of BiVO4 (vide infra). In order to avoid a higher mismatch in the heterojunctions, the cell parameters of all these species were kept similar. Geometries of all these species were optimized at generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange–correlation functional and double Zeta Polarized (DZP) basis set [24]. Linear combination of atomic orbitals (LCAO) method has been employed for Bi, V, C, N, H, and O atoms [25]. Different types and sizes of the Monkhorst-Pack k-grid and energy cutoff were employed for each individual sample. The TB09LDA functional of meta-GGA was performed to estimate the band structure properties by embedding with the Tran and Blaha XC functional equation [25]. Furthermore, we also measured the DOS, PDOS, band structure, EDD, electrostatic potential, and effective masses of the charge carriers according to the protocols described by Endres et al. [26].

Fig. 1 (a) manifests the Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra of all of the as-developed photocatalyst. The FTIR spectra of the pure BiVO4 and g-C3N4 sample has been comprehensively discussed in our previous publication [21,27]. Generally, the pure BiVO4 was made up of a broad peak and weak shoulder located around 750 cm−1 (v3 of the VO4 3-) and 530 cm−1 (v4 of the VO4 3-) [17]. Moreover, the observed broad and small FTIR peaks monitored at 3460 and 1500 cm−1, respectively were stemming from the OH stretching and OH bending mode, respectively [28]. The presence of the g-C3N4 photocatalyst was verified through the detection of the triazine units which can be detected at 813 cm−1 [29].

Furthermore, the multiple peaks in the range 1300–1500 cm−1 corresponds to the typical stretching modes of CN heterocycles [30]. The small peak located at 1240 and 1317 cm−1 were attributed to the partial condensation of CNHC and full condensation of CN(C)C units, respectively. It is conspicuous that the heterostructure samples exemplified identical characteristic peaks of parent BiVO4 and g-C3N4, highlighting that the BiVO4/g-C3N4 heterostructure was synthesized successfully. Interestingly, the FTIR patterns for the binary and ternary structure samples were almost identical with no evident signal of RGO and GQDs were detected. These similar FTIR patterns among the binary and ternary samples can be occurred due to the low loading amount of graphene-based materials used in this study.

The XRD analysis revealed that the photocatalyst sample feature two different crystallographic properties which were tetragonal and monoclinic structure, corresponds to the BiVO4 properties as shown in Fig. 1 (b). The peaks of the monoclinic structure located at 10.2, 10.9, 30.6, 31.5 and 32.6° of 2θ while the peaks of the tetragonal structure located at 24.6 and 25.3° of 2θ. Both crystal structures can be indexed to the JCPDS card no. 14-0688 and JCPDS card no. 14-0133 [21]. Meanwhile, peak detected at 28.5° of 2θ was belongs to the (002) facet of the g-C3N4 [31]. Nevertheless, there are no XRD diffraction peaks appeared corresponded to the RGO and GQDs, presumably due to the low content and high dispersity in the heterostructure sample.

The morphological structures of the binary and ternary heterostructure photocatalysts were scrutinized using FESEM and TEM. As depicted in Fig. 2 , the FESEM images revealed that the BiVO4 possesses the typical microflower shape like structure with distinctly sharp edges. Notably, the crumple two-dimensional (2D) porous structure was randomly distributed on the surface of the BiVO4 microflower corresponded to the g-C3N4, manifesting the intimate contact between BiVO4 and g-C3N4 particles. The inset FESEM image in Fig. 2 (b) indicates the presence of the wrinkled thin-silk structure of RGO originated from the decomposition of the oxygen functional groups that leads to graphene-like sheets with a disordered stacking [32]. The detection of RGO in the heterostructure BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 sample signifies the successful fabrication of the composite sample.

Similarly, the BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 sample exemplifies the typical identical morphological structure of individual BiVO4 and g-C3N4. However, there is no morphological structure corresponded to GQD was detected in the FESEM micrograph image, presumably due to the small size. Fig. 2 (d) exhibits the HRTEM image of the as-developed GQDs which have a diameter range of 5–9 nm. The lattices of GQD can be clearly observed, attaining a high crystallization level of GQDs. The interplanar spacing of GQDs is 0.21 nm which coincides well with typical (101) spacing of graphitic carbon [33,34]. Furthermore, the top and cross-sectional FESEM images along with the EDX and elemental mapping of the BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 and BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 were demonstrated in Figs. S2 and S3 which clearly suggesting the presence of all elemental footprints corresponds to the parental BiVO4, RGO, GQD and g-C3N4 sample.

Previously, we report that the pure BiVO4 and g-C3N4 photocatalyst possesses an absorption edge around 520 and 430 nm, conforming to bandgap energy of 2.40 and 2.80 eV, respectively [35–38]. Meanwhile, the binary BiVO4/g-C3N4 exhibits slightly increased in absorption intensity at 530 nm as shown in Fig. 3 (a), evincing that the hybridization of individual photocatalyst does improve the light absorption capacity near the visible-light region. On the other hand, both of the ternary heterostructure samples manifest an almost similar absorption edge in comparison to the binary structure. Based on Fig. 3 (b), it was estimated that the bandgap energy of BiVO4/g-C3N4, BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 and BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 samples were calculated to be 2.74, 2.52 and 2.47 eV. It is worth noting that the addition of RGO and GQDs as an electron mediator further enhanced the absorption capacity of the composite materials near the visible-light region, results in significant enhancement on the photoelectrocatalytic performance. Moreover, the RGO and GQDs can act as a photosensitizer for bandgap narrowing, endowing efficient electron-hole pairs separation and hindered the photocharge carrier recombination.

The performance of the binary and ternary heterostructure samples was evaluated using natural lake water as illustrated in Fig. 3 (c). There is no chemical electrolyte was added during the measurement in order to highlight the potential of green hydrogen production via natural water sources. Previously, our group reported that the photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen performance of the pure BiVO4 and g-C3N4 were found to be 9.5 and 11.9 mmol/h [21]. The low photoelectrocatalytic performance for pure samples was stemming from its natural limitation which is the fast recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carriers [6,19]. Interestingly, the hybridization of BiVO4 and g-C3N4 to form the heterostructure system significantly enhanced photoelectrocatalytic activity despite being without any sacrificial reagents. Moreover, the addition of RGO in the ternary heterostructure system has a substantial effect on the performance compared to the binary system. The high photoelectrochemical hydrogen production of 63.5 mmol demonstrated by the BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 sample implying that the RGO serves as an excellent electron mediator for the reduction process which lowers the photocatalytic overpotential and thus minimizing the photocharge carrier recombination [39]. Moreover, the intimate interfacial interaction between the individual photocatalyst with the RGO, evidently from the FESEM micrograph image does allow a smooth and efficient photocharge carrier separation and migration between the adjacent photocatalyst. This phenomenon is aligned with principle akin to other reported studies, whereby the addition of the RGO does help in boosting the photocatalytic activity [17,40,41].

Interestingly, the formation of the ternary heterostructure with the GQDs material demonstrates the highest photoelectrocatalytic activity of 84.9 mmol/h which was ~25% higher than the composite sample with RGO as an electron mediator. This scenario could be attributed to the strong quantum confinement effect and boundary effect of GQDs owing to its smaller size below 10 nm in comparison to the RGO which typically size range below 100 nm [42]. Moreover, the hybridization of GQDs in the composite heterostructure system not only promotes the π-electron delocalization, but also creates a new photocharge carrier separation and migration pathways with lower energy, results in enhancement of visible-light harvesting [43].

The EIS analysis was used to examine the properties of the photocharge carrier separation and migration within the binary and ternary structure as shown in Fig. 3 (d). Generally, the semicircle arc radius on the EIS Nyquist plot indicating the photocharge carrier resistance in which the bigger the arc radii, the higher the resistance, consequently jeopardizing the photocatalytic performance. As reported in our work previously [21,27,44], the EIS Nyquist plot for the pure BiVO4 and g-C3N4 shows a bigger semicircle arc diameter with high photocharge carrier resistance due to its inherent limitation as single photocatalyst materials [45]. However, the observed semicircle arc diameter of the ternary heterostructure possesses a small diameter than the binary heterostructure. This observation signifying the robust interfacial photocharge carrier migration and separation through the ternary heterostructure with the aids of either RGO or GQDs. Comparatively, the BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 sample exhibit the smallest semicircle arc diameter than BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 sample, indicating that the GQD serve better as an electron mediator at the interface heterojunction than RGO.

The current–potential (I-V) curve of the studied photocatalyst was demonstrated in Fig. 3 (e). The binary BiVO4/g-C3N4 attained a photocurrent density of 9.62 mA/cm2 which is higher than the pure sample as reported by our group previously [21]. Interestingly, the photocurrent density of the binary BiVO4/g-C3N4 sample integrated either with RGO or GQD was considerably higher relative to the sample without an electron mediator, signifying that the ternary heterostructure sample has excellent photocharge carrier separation and migration upon light illumination. In addition, the ternary heterostructure embedded with the RGO achieved a photocurrent density of 14.6 mA/cm2 while the ternary heterostructure sample integrated with the GQD exhibit the highest photocurrent density of 19.2 mA/cm2 at 1.0 V vs Ag/AgCl. The enhanced by 24% of the photocurrent density by composite sample integrated with GQD compare to RGO further corroborated the EIS Nyquist analysis that the GQD serves better as an electron mediator at the interface heterojunction with a minimal photocharge carrier resistance.

The ABPE was measured to examine the relationship between the applied voltage and photocurrent density and the details on the ABPE can be referred elsewhere [8,38,45]. Previously, our group reported that the ABPE of the pure BiVO4 was only 0.056% in which confirming the limited photocatalytic nature of the single photocatalyst. However, after constructing the binary system with g-C3N4, the ABPE of this sample reaches 0.29%, indicating an 80% improvement in the ABPE when BiVO4 has formed a heterostructure system with the g-C3N4. Meanwhile, the formation of the ternary heterostructure sample with the RGO demonstrates superior ABPE with 0.40% efficiency at −0.03 V vs. Ag/AgCl. Concomitant with this, the ternary heterostructure sample embedded with the GQDs displays the highest photoconversion efficiency (0.57%) in comparison to other samples as illustrates in Fig. 3 (f). Such results can be ascribed to the decrease in the photocharge resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface induced by the quantum size of GQD, evidently from the EIS Nyquist plot. Furthermore, the hybridization of GQD not only improved the ABPE of the composite BiVO4/g-C3N4 sample, but also significantly improved the resistance of the as-developed photocatalyst against photocorrosion, affirming it position as a frontier photocatalyst for a wide range of photocatalytic applications.

The stability and recyclability analysis of the BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 was essential for demonstrating its practical applications as the photocorrosion is one of the major limiting factors that affecting the photocatalytic activity. Fig. 4 (a) demonstrates the stability and recyclability performance of the ternary heterostructure sample for three cyclic activity. It can be seen that the ternary heterostructure sample manifests an excellent photostability with only a 3.6% loss in comparison to the first cycle. The minimal loss in the hydrogen evolution performance inferred on the great BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 stability against photocorrosion. Furthermore, we further justified the excellent photostability of this ternary heterostructure by comparing its XRD analysis before and after the performance testing. Fig. 4 (b) shows that there is no noticeable change in the crystallographic structure of the BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 sample was monitored in comparison to the fresh sample even after three cyclic activity. These results further corroborated that the as-developed BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 sample possesses exceptional stability against photocorrosion in which signifying the practicality of this developed material.

As discussed in the experimental section, the ternary BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 sample was superior to the RGO-ternary heterostructure. So, in order to correlate and confirm our observation; we performed DFT analysis for bare samples including BiVO4, g-C3N4, RGO, GQD, and also for the binary and ternary heterostructure samples which include BiVO4/g-C3N4, BiVO4/GQD, BiVO4/RGO, BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 and BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 heterostructures. The optimized relaxed structures of the investigated models are given in Fig. 5 . The positive value (1.95 J/m2) monitored from the surface formation energy of BiVO4 indicates that the sample retains a stable and non-polar characteristic [46]. Thus, the slab of BiVO4 was chosen to construct the mentioned binary and ternary heterostructures, with almost no lattice mismatch (0.02%).

The successful incorporation of RGO, GQD, and g-C3N4 on the surface of BiVO4 was confirmed from their strong interaction energy, charge transfer, and electrostatic potential map. These can be seen from a strong electrostatic interaction of a non-covalent nature that was developed with the BiN, BiC, OC, ON and HO inter-bonding. Correspondingly, the calculated interaction/adsorption energy of these interactions was in the range of −0.58 to −0.83 eV, indicate the binary and ternary heterostructures were thermodynamically stable. where E(Product), EReactant 1, and EReactant 2 are the sum of the energy of the relaxed composite system, layer(s) of RGO, GQD, and g-C3N4, and BiVO4, respectively. Comparative analysis of the electrostatic potential maps of all the investigated species led us to predict that BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 was more stable than others. This can be seen from its homogeneous distribution of electrostatic potential maps, especially at the interface as shown in Fig. 6 .

In addition, the inter-charge transfer within both ternary heterostructure samples was estimated with the help of charge density difference (CDD), and the results were shown in Fig. 7 , while that of other species was shown in Figs. S4 and S5. Based on Fig. 7, there are two areas under the curve was coloured in yellow which corresponds to the accumulation of the charge. Meanwhile, the green coloured curve represents the depletion charge region. It can be seen that the charge distribution of both ternary heterostructure samples dominantly occurs at the interface region. However, there was no accumulation and depletion region were observed on BiVO4; especially those parts which were far away from the interface. This phenomenon is important as it was responsible for creating an electric field at the heterostructure interface, results in enhancing the separation and migration of the photocharge carriers (vide supra). Moreover, the observed charge distribution patterns indicate the formation of a weak Vander Waal type interaction between the individual samples [47]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the studied ternary heterostructure system possesses a typical p-n heterojunction system [48].

On the other hand, a slice of the planar-averaged CDD for both the ternary heterostructure system, along with the Z-direction was illustrated at the bottom of Fig. 7. Interestingly, the individual photocatalysts in the ternary BiVO4/GQDs/g-C3N4 heterostructure shared their electronic cloud density through orbital hybridizations. Whilst, the electronic cloud density map of RGO, g-C3N4 and BiVO4 in BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 heterostructure, show less mutual sharing, especially between RGO and g-C3N4. This obvious observation signifies that the GQDs and g-C3N4 form a more stable and stronger heterojunction with BiVO4 (BiVO4/GQDs/g-C3N4), as evident from its degenerate CDD plot. Moreover, the charge transferring phenomena occurring in this GQDs-ternary heterostructure was higher than the RGO-ternary heterojunction (Fig. 7). This statement nicely correlates our experimental data where the GQDs significantly enhanced the photoelectrocatalytic performance than RGO in the ternary heterostructure system.

In order to correlate and confirm the high performance of our experimentally observed BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 photocatalyst, we calculated the band structure and PDOS of the mentioned models. Moreover, the energy bands of a single layer of individual bare samples were also calculated which were depicted in Figs. S6–S11 of the Supporting Information. The simulated bandgaps of BiVO4, BiVO4/g-C3N4, BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 and BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 were 2.46, 2.46, 2.40, and 2.45 eV, respectively (shown in Fig. S6 and Table 1 ). These simulated bandgaps have a good correlation with our experimentally observed bandgaps of 2.40 eV for BiVO4, 2.74 eV for BiVO4/g-C3N4, 2.52 eV for BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4, and 2.47 eV for BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4. It was found that the theoretical data were in accordance with the experimental data which further justified our theoretical protocol as well as validating the significant enhancement of photoelectrocatalytic activity boosted by GQDs.

The work function, valence band maximum (VBM), conduction band minimum (CBM), effective masses of electrons and holes of the investigated species were listed in Table 1. BiVO4 has an indirect bandgap of 2.46 eV which remains similar upon interaction with g-C3N4 in the binary heterostructure. However, the direction of the Brillouin zone was changed due to the different geometrical structures of the binary heterostructure. From Fig. S10, we can see that upon the interaction of g-C3N4, RGO, and GQD with BiVO4; extra flat bands were produced in the VB of the ternary heterostructure system. The additional of these flat bands results in the formation of the holes trapping centres which was responsible for minimizing the photocharge carrier recombination. The original VB and CB of BiVO4 in these ternary heterostructures were highlighted with blue and red lines, respectively (Figs. S10 and S11). Correspondingly, we can safely infer that the enhanced photocatalytic activity and high photocharge carrier mobility were stemming from these flat bands (vide supra). Additionally, the C and N atoms of RGO, GQD, and g-C3N4, were also contributing to the formation of these additional flat bands (vide infra).

On the other hand, the effective masses (me* and mh*) of the photogenerated charge carriers were measured by fitting the parabolic estimation at both CBM and VBM. The estimation is measured according to Eq. (2) where the details of this equation have been discussed previously [23,46].

Table 1 summarizes the details of DFT calculations for the effective masses of the photocharge carriers along with the work function (θ), Water molecule adsorption energy (EAd) and other parameters. Theoretically, the lighter the charge carriers’ properties along with the larger difference between both effective masses, the more efficient will the photocharge carriers be separated and migrated, thus augmented the overall photoelectrocatalytic performance. According to the DFT calculations tabulated in Table 1, it can be inferred that the effective masses of photogenerated electrons and holes for bare sample, binary and ternary heterostructure systems were substantially lighter, thus responsible for efficient photocatalytic activity. Among all of the studied samples, the BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 can be considered as the most efficient material owing to its larger difference of 0.45 me. This calculation was well aligned with our experimental findings as being discussed above.

It should be noted that the partial density of state (PDOS) is responsible for the contribution of VBM and CBM. The PDOS of the studied binary and ternary heterostructure was examined and the obtained results were illustrated in Fig. 8 as well as in Figs. S12 and S13 of the Supporting Information. From Fig. 8 (a), the VBM and CBM of the BiVO4 were composed of the p orbitals originated from the O atoms and 5d orbitals originated from the V atoms, respectively. These VBM and CBM were located at −7.03 and −4.57 eV at vacuum levels. Nevertheless, the VBM of the binary heterostructure system was mainly constituted by the p orbitals of N atoms, responsible for flat bands (vide supra). It is noteworthy to mention that the PDOS of N atoms, especially near the VBM was not hybridized with the orbitals of O, C, Bi, and V atoms. In fact, the bands of N at VBM have appeared as separate states which do not increase the overall stability and long-term catalytic activity of binary heterostructure sample. Nonetheless, the CBM of the binary heterostructure system does not change but the density of states of V was reduced compared to that of BiVO4.

On the other hand, the PDOS analysis of the ternary heterostructure systems indicates that the orbital hybridization of N atom with O, C, Bi, and V atoms was comparatively stronger than the binary heterostructure photocatalyst as shown in Fig. 8 (c and d). Correspondingly, the orbitals of N, Bi, V, C, and O atoms in the VB of the GQD-ternary heterostructure system depicts the strongest hybridization than the other studied photocatalysts. Thus, it can be said that the GQD, g-C3N4, and BiVO4 in the ternary BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 system has strong overlapping, responsible for an efficient photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen production. This statement strongly corroborates our experimental observation as well.

The Schematic illustration of the band edge positions for all photocatalysts was shown in Fig. 9 . The band alignment of the investigated species was calculated according to Eq. (3).

The simulated work functions of BiVO4, binary heterostructure photocatalyst, RGO-ternary heterostructure photocatalyst and GQDs-ternary heterostructure photocatalyst were 5.18, 4.43, 4.30, and 4.21 eV, respectively (refer Table 1 and Table S1). The simulated work functions suggest that the incorporation of the carbonaceous materials in the ternary heterostructure system improved the photocharge carrier’s mobility across the heterostructure interface. Additionally, the obtained work function suggests that the photocharge carrier was moved towards the BiVO4 until the Fermi energy of these units were coordinated.

From Fig. 9, we can see that the VBM of BiVO4 was higher than the binary and ternary heterostructure photocatalysts. Similarly, the CBM of the BiVO4 was at lower potential (vs. vacuum) than the other studied photocatalysts. Nevertheless, the formation of the heterostructure system has shifted the Fermi energy and the band edge positions to become optimum compared to the individual entities (see Table S1). This shifting position is essential for establishing the internal electric field within the heterostructure system that is responsible for improving the overall photoelectrocatalytic activity. Surprisingly, the change in Fermi energy level (4.21 eV vs. vacuum) and band edge potentials of BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 were more prominent than the other studied photocatalysts. Thus, this observation further supported that the boosted photoelectrocatalytic performance of the BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 was mainly due to the ideal position of its VB and CB (straddle with the redox potential of water), and a larger difference between the effective masses of the photocharge carriers (0.45 me). As the location of the CB for this ternary heterostructure was higher than the standard H+/H2 at the vacuum level, more H+ were accessible for the reaction to occur, thus a significant amount of H+ can be converted into H2. Accordingly, as the CB of this ternary heterostructure photocatalyst was more negative than the standard O2/H2O, more water molecules can be oxidized into O2.

Finally, we interact water molecules over the surfaces of the studied photocatalysts systems, and allowed to relax, to find the overpotential for water. The optimized relaxed geometries were depicted in Fig. 10 and Fig. S14, while the per water molecule adsorption energy over the surfaces of investigated species was listed in Table 1. Again, it was found that BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 has a higher potential for the water molecule, where the per water molecule adsorption energy was −3.42 eV. As shown in Fig. 10, some of the water molecules were repelled in the BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4 system which consequently decreased the adsorption energy of water and reduce its catalytic activity. The per water molecule adsorption energy of BiVO4@H2O, BiVO4/g-C3N4@H2O, and BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4@H2O were −2.52, −2.84, and −3.02 eV, respectively.

In summation, the effects of RGO and GQDs as electron mediators on the photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen production of BiVO4/g-C3N4 have been studied and further corroborated and counterchecked with DFT analysis. The obtained experimental and computational analyses suggest that the GQDs serves as an excellent electron mediator than RGO within the ternary heterostructure system. Additionally, the ternary BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 possesses the highest photocurrent density in which ~24% higher than the BiVO4/g-C3N4 integrated with RGO. Moreover, the BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 sample showed the highest photoconversion efficiency in which ~90% enhancement in the efficiency in comparison to the pure sample. Our theoretical simulations strongly support the experimental performance, where it suggests that the atomic orbitals of GQD form strong overlapping with the BiVO4 and g-C3N4. Additionally, the ternary BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 was found to be more stable and have higher interaction energy with a water molecule (−3.42 eV), compared to BiVO4/RGO/g-C3N4. Thus, the aforementioned features possess by the ternary BiVO4/GQD/g-C3N4 were responsible for boosting the photoelectrocatalytic performance. Thus, for the first time, it was revealed that the addition of GQD in the BiVO4/g-C3N4 heterostructure system promotes better photocharge carrier separation and migration than RGO with higher ABPE. This finding offers a new paradigm in the view of the utilization of an electron mediator from the carbonaceous materials in which GQD can be regarded as a more efficient electron mediator than the RGO.
